metadata.title: Own Your Home: Launch a Brand-Aligned Substack
metadata.playlist: Build a Home — Thought Leadership on Substack
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 10 brief — Self-Promotion Through Thought Leadership

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 1 of 5. In Assignment 9 you published one article on LinkedIn — a borrowed platform. This assignment is different. A Substack is a home: a publication you own, a subscriber list you control, a voice that compounds over time. LinkedIn can change its algorithm and bury your post. Your Substack URL, your subscriber email list — those are yours. This episode sets up the publication. Five elements: name, tagline, About page, logo and header from your existing brand assets, and a custom URL. One note: this assignment's natural drafting engine is the skep plugin — skep:about, skep:manifesto, skep:essay. The Your Turn prompts in this series route you there. Let's go.

props.segment: Own Your Home: Launch a Brand-Aligned Substack
props.command: 5 setup elements: name · tagline · About page (150–200 words) · logo/header from A7–8 · custom URL.
props.runningText: owned beats borrowed — subscriber list you control, URL that's yours, voice that compounds…
output: About page 150–200 words from A6 brand + A9 story

beats.act: owned beats borrowed — why this matters
beats.narration_text: Why a Substack instead of publishing everything on LinkedIn. Three reasons. First: a Substack is indexed by search engines — an article you publish here accumulates authority over time, not just for a day or two before the algorithm moves on. Second: the subscriber list is yours. When someone subscribes to your Substack, you have their email address. If LinkedIn changes its algorithm, if a platform shuts down, the list comes with you. Third: a Substack compounds. The first article is not the point — the publication is. Hiring managers and collaborators who find you through a Substack see a body of work, a consistent voice, a publication with a clear perspective. That is a very different signal than a list of one-off posts on a borrowed feed. Assignment 9 was about writing one story. Assignment 10 is about building the place that story lives — and where the next ten stories will live. The relationship between owned and borrowed media is not either-or: you publish on LinkedIn to drive traffic to the Substack, and the Substack is where people who want more come home to.

props.artifactHeading: 3 reasons a Substack > LinkedIn for this
artifactLines: 1. Indexed: search engines accumulate authority over time — not buried by an algorithm next week.
2. Subscriber list is yours: email addresses survive platform changes.
3. Compounds: a body of work + consistent voice = a different hiring signal than one-off posts.
LinkedIn drives traffic to the Substack. Substack is where people who want more come home.
A9 = one story. A10 = the place that story lives, and where the next 10 will live.
props.sparkLine: Owned media compounds. Borrowed media rents. Build the home.

beats.narration_text: Five elements to set up. One: the name. Personal brand: your name or a variant of it — FirstName LastName, or a pen name you have been using. Startup brand: the tool name from Assignment 6, or a closely related publication name. The name should be recognizable to anyone who has already encountered your brand in A7 or A8. Two: the tagline. One sentence that tells a first-time reader exactly what they get. Not 'thoughts on technology' — 'weekly AI pipeline insights for analysts who want to stop trusting black-box output.' Specific enough that the right reader knows immediately and the wrong reader knows immediately. Test it with the read-aloud gut-check from Assignment 6. Three: the About page, one hundred fifty to two hundred words — what the publication is, who it is for, what they get, and why you are the one writing it. The brand foundation from Assignment 6 is the source material. The A9 story is the proof of credibility. Four: the logo and header image. Use the assets from Assignment 7 or 8 — the same brand family as your LinkedIn header and your website. Same palette, same font treatment, clearly related. Do not use Substack's default gradient. Five: the custom URL. Set it to yourname.substack.com — or your tool name. This is the address on the publication's home. Set it before the first article publishes.

props.artifactHeading: Name · Tagline · About · Logo/Header · Custom URL
artifactLines: Name: personal name or tool brand. Recognizable to anyone who's seen A7/A8.
Tagline: 1 sentence — what readers get, specific enough to filter wrong readers out.
About page: 150–200 words — what it is · who it's for · what they get · why you.
Logo/header: A7/A8 assets — same brand family. Not Substack's default gradient.
Custom URL: yourname.substack.com — set it before publishing the first article.
props.sparkLine: 5 elements. All 5 before the first article. Tagline is the hardest — draft it first.

beats.act: the about page — what it must do
beats.narration_text: The About page is the first thing a potential subscriber reads when they are deciding whether to follow. It has to do four things in one hundred fifty to two hundred words. One: state what the publication is — the subject, the format, the frequency. 'A weekly newsletter for data practitioners building AI pipelines, covering what goes wrong and how to build pipelines that fail gracefully.' Two: state who it is for — a specific reader, not everyone. 'Written for analysts and engineers who are deploying AI in production and want to stop trusting output nobody reviewed.' Three: what they get from subscribing — a specific, concrete promise. 'Every issue includes one lesson from a real pipeline run, one auditable example, and one question to test your own setup.' Four: why you — a one to two sentence credibility signal drawn from your A9 story. Not 'I am passionate about AI' — 'I built a pipeline for a class assignment that ran for two weeks before I found a quality gate that was checking the wrong field. That is the kind of thing this newsletter is about.' The skep:about skill or the Your Turn prompt generates a draft from your A6 brand + A9 story. Then the voice pass: same two tests as Episode 3 of A9 — every sentence that could be written by anyone needs a specific detail, every word you would never say needs to go.

props.artifactHeading: What · Who · What they get · Why you
artifactLines: What: subject + format + frequency. Specific, not 'thoughts on technology.'
Who: a specific reader — not everyone. Name the situation or role.
What they get: a concrete promise per issue — one lesson, one example, one question.
Why you: 1–2 sentences from your A9 credibility story — real situation, not 'I'm passionate about AI.'
skep:about drafts from A6 brand + A9 story → then the voice pass.
props.sparkLine: 4 jobs. 150–200 words. Credibility comes from the A9 story, not a claim.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Launch a Brand-Aligned Substack. Owned beats borrowed: indexed, subscriber list is yours, compounds over time. Five setup elements before the first article: name recognizable from A7 and A8, tagline that filters the right reader in and the wrong reader out, About page with four jobs in one hundred fifty to two hundred words, logo and header from your existing brand assets, and custom URL set at yourname.substack.com. The About page credibility comes from the A9 story — a real situation, not a claim. The skep:about skill or the Your Turn prompt drafts the About page; then the voice pass. Do not use Substack's default gradient. Episode 2 is the brand consistency audit.

props.artifactTitle: Own Your Home: Launch a Brand-Aligned Substack
artifactLines: 5 elements: name · tagline (filters right reader) · About page (what/who/what they get/why you).
Logo/header from A7/A8 — same brand family. No default Substack gradient.
Custom URL: yourname.substack.com — set before first article.
About credibility: A9 real story, not 'I'm passionate about AI.'
skep:about → draft → voice pass. Episode 2 = brand consistency audit.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. The prompt to draft the About page. Paste it into Claude or into the skep:about skill if you have access. The prompt reads: I am writing the About page for a Substack publication for INFO 7375 Assignment 10. Publication name: [name]. It is a [personal brand / startup brand for the tool called (tool name)]. Based on my Assignment 6 brand foundation: the Mission is [paste it], the primary user is [paste the primary_user field from the brand_config.json or brand foundation], and the Differentiation pillar is [paste it]. From Assignment 9, the story that establishes my credibility is: [paste the A9 turned-point sentence and the resolution]. Write a 150–200 word About page with these four sections, in this order: what the publication covers and how often; who it is for, described specifically; what a subscriber gets per issue as a concrete promise; and a one-to-two sentence credibility statement built from the A9 story I gave you. Do not use any phrases like 'passionate about,' 'I believe in,' 'leverage,' or 'driving value.' Write in the first person, in a direct voice. After you get the draft, apply the voice pass: read every sentence aloud. Replace any sentence that could apply to a different publication with a detail that could only apply to yours. That About page is the front door to everything else in this assignment.

props.segment: Own Your Home: Launch a Brand-Aligned Substack
props.command: Publication name: [name]. Mission: [paste]. Primary user: [paste]. Differentiation: [paste]. A9 turning point + resolution: [paste]. Draft 150–200 word About: what · who (specific) · what they get per issue (concrete) · credibility (A9 story). No filler. Voice pass before you publish.
props.runningText: the About page is the front door — replace any sentence that could describe a different publication…
